speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, we're going to get-- we're-- OK. It's February 26, 1995. This is an interview with Lillian Eckstein. I am Amy Friedman, the interviewer. We are in New York, in the United States of America. The interview is in English. And we are on tape four. OK. Mrs. Eckstein, you were saying that you had typhoid at Bergen-Belsen. Do you have any idea how long you were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Bergen-Belsen? No. I don't-- I never talk about it. I don't want to think about it, because it interferes with my daily living here. I want to be able to live in control my emotions. Because you cannot continue living in the past. So I, I managed to shut out the life that I have led throughout my life. Because when I came to this country, , I decided that I had to start all over again. And I am going to be like everybody else, despite of the fact that I had constantly nightmares, fear of people, feeling inferior, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] not as good as the next one. And, this unfortunately, had continued for the rest of my life. No matter how hard I try to cover up my pain, it's there. It's I am, I am not very good at it lately, lately that I am retired. I have a little more time on my hands. And I just now realize I have time to think. My life is coming towards an end. What happened, it's a nightmare [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that it's beyond belief. I sometimes wake up and sweat and cry or scream, because I'm afraid. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I was hoping to be able to get some help for this. But what can anybody do to you the facts that I have lived through? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So I just exist and go on with life. Depression, loneliness, fear-- it's not a very good feeling. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I hope I don't have much time to go, because it's very hard on me. It's hard on me for many reasons.

speaker: interviewee
text: I want to be able to [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] give my parents' names. My older daughter is named after my mother. My father had no name. So one of the rabbis in [INAUDIBLE] Park gave me a name for my little boy. So my father has a name, too. That means a lot to me. My husband's mother is named after our younger daughter. So my mother, my father, my mother-in-law, and my father-in-law have names.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you realize that liberation was coming at Bergen-Belsen?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was laying down there. I felt a very cold body near me. And that person was dead. And I-- there was four observation towers in Bergen-Belsen. And I looked up. And I saw one of them, there was no German with a gun there. So I figured, I said to myself, I must be hallucinating. And we heard bombings and shootings. But at that point, it didn't matter to us what we heard. But we did not realize that the army is coming closer. And when I realized that there was no German up in the observation tower for a long time, that's when I realized that the Germans were running with their hands up towards the exit of the camp. And the English army was marching in. Well, the horror in their eyes are unbelievable when they saw the mountains, mountains of dead people. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] They were drawing crosses on their faces when they saw that. And that was our of freedom.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened, literally, to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know what happened. I wound up-- woke up in a hospital. I don't know how I got there-- was there a few weeks. And I didn't speak English those days, so I spoke fluently German and Yiddish. And so there was some social worker or, or a volunteer, thanks to you people, who tried to interview me or ask me who I was and where I was born and when I was born. And I didn't remember. I didn't remember. It was very embarrassing. I did not remember anything. So they just marked down whatever. I knew, I knew that I was born in Czechoslovakia, in Nizni Verecky. But I-- after awhile, they were feeding us. And I came to myself. And I then decided to see who was around.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was your sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister, who was with me, was taken to Sweden because she had typhoid-- she had tuberculosis. And it was very contagious. So they took her to Sweden in a sanatorium. And that's where she lived.

speaker: interviewer
text: So at that point, you were alone. You were alone. You woke up in the hospital.

speaker: interviewee
text: I w-- that was Ella who came from Budapest. I tried to-- I didn't catch the whole thing. Ella came from Budapest to Bergen-Belsen. They-- she went from Czechoslovakia to Budapest. And she lived in Budapest. And from Budapest, they took the Hungarian Jews to Bergen-Belsen. I was with my sister, who is here in Queens-- excuse me-- who can't-- who couldn't be here today, because she's ill. I was with her. And when they took Ella to Sweden, I didn't know at that time even where they're taking her. Because I, I was like, I was like a zombie. I just didn't know what was going on anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us about the reunion? So most of the war, that sister was away. But you reunited in Bergen-Belsen?

speaker: interviewee
text: She w-- Yes. She was--

speaker: interviewer
text: How was that? Tell us about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: This-- someone told me that my sister, Ella, is in a bunk number twelve. So I couldn't believe that they took the people from Budapest. Because we didn't know that they're taking the Hungary Jews also. So they took the Hungarian Jews straight to Bergen-Belsen. And that's where I found her.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us about the meeting.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was no emotions. There was no emotion. We didn't-- we, we just looked at each other. And I felt a million miles away because I didn't-- there was no feelings left. We were not human. We had no human feelings left.

speaker: interviewee
text: So she's there, so she's there. So I asked her, how did you wind up here? So she told us that they had took Hungarian Jews straight to Bergen-Belsen. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And so after many years, I brought her to the United States. And she lives in Manhattan, Ella. So you were in the hospital. And you woke up. And you got your memory back. Then what

speaker: interviewee
text: Didn't get my memory back.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh.

speaker: interviewee
text: Until this day, I struggle to remember things, until this day. I went-- they gave us train passes to go. We went on the train passes. We drive-- we went all over to look for the families. So I went to Prague, to Munich, by train. And there was no connections to go. So I stayed at the train station for a few days, sleeping on the train like they sleep in the Grand Central Station, the homeless people. And from there, we went to Prague by train. And then we went home by train. And we couldn't find anybody. So I returned back to Germany to displaced camps so we can leave Germany. This was the only place where they have displaced camps. And Vienna and in Munich, we went to Munich. And I waited four years to come to the United States in displaced camps.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of that place?

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Hum ?] [? Finck ?] by [? Mildorf ?] that's near Munich. And that's where they, they interview you. They make sure that you're physically and emotionally well. Otherwise, you couldn't come into this country. They examine you physically. And they test you emotionally. And with the help of the United Jewish Appeal, with the help of my uncle who lived in the Bronx. I came to this country 1949. I arrived Monday. And I-- Thursday, I applied for school. I went to school in Monroe night school in the Bronx, where I went for three years. And in 1950, I met Michael. I married Michael Eckstein. And we settled in the Bronx. Worked in a sweat shop in the daytime. And at night, we went to school. And 1953, Marilyn was born, In 1956, Irene was born. And we lived in the Bronx for 25 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Life in the Bronx-- we lived in two rooms in Minford Place there, East Bronx. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We were quite happy. We had a home. We had food. And we went to school. And we were very devoted to each other. The children went to school. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: We realized that both of the girls are quite smart. And so we decided that perhaps she-- they should go-- become in the medical profession. But those days, it was impossible for girls to be in medical school, right? So Marilyn became a schoolteacher. She attended Yeshiva Hebrew Academy, Acad-- Akiba Hebrew. Academy, and Yeshiva university. At the age of 22, she received her master's and bachelor's in education.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then a year later, she decided she wants to go into medicine. And she applied to medical school. And she made it. She now is an infectious disease specialist in Dallas, Texas in [? Parkland ?] Hospital. Irene is a registered nurse. She got her education at Beth-Israel Hospital in Manhattan. She's a trauma nurse and works at The Village at St. Vincent's Hospital. They're both married. They both have, each of them, two children. Irene is expecting a third one. And we're very happy for-- with the, with the children We're very close with our daughters. And we're very proud of them, that they have accomplished something and contributes to society. And our time is coming to a close. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I just hope and pray that such catastrophe, disaster for no reason at all, should never happen again.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did your experience affect the rest of your life, do you think, raising your kids?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was very difficult. And there were times that we had problems financially. We couldn't pay the rent. We-- our rent was $35 a month. So we managed to-- we had to buy yesterday's bread because it was a nickel cheaper. I sewed all the dresses. I made the clothes for my children. And we did not live. I used to, used to put a white tablecloth on Shabbos. But there wasn't much food on the table. But nobody knew. We didn't use welfare. We didn't know. And we were too proud to something like that. Through hard work, through devotion to each other, we're here.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you say that you do Shabbos? You did Shabbos?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes, we did. We did it, we did it for our children's sake. Because I want them to know that they are human beings. Without any kind of religion, you live an empty life. I remember the, the [? Shabbos ?] [? home, ?] the life, home, the holidays.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was something to look forward to. It was something to celebrate. We were, we were counting the minutes they should light the candles on Sabbath. There was something to, to enjoy. And without this, what is there in life? I found today, after all this, going through these periods in life, that life is quite empty. And I ask myself, why? What-- I would do anything in life if anybody could help me to get rid of these horrible feelings, these horrible [INAUDIBLE], that there is something. But there was a time when I used to ask God, where are you? Where-- why aren't you helping us? Why? And there was silence. And sometimes I began to wonder, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] what is there? Why? Why did they make us to be such a empty shell-- not believing. What did they do to us? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It's not a malignancy. It is something that they, they took away the human feelings from us. We pretended all these years.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when I'm with my grandchildren, I pretend. I humor them. They look up to me. They, they look up to me, that I'm a giant. And I'm really dead, empty, empty, emotionally disturbed. I'm not sick emotionally. I am just very broken, dead.

speaker: interviewee
text: Nobody believes me. I was nominated Woman of the Year. I have plaques to show you. I was modeling clothes. Nobody knew that I, I walk in the dark. I walk, I walk in the shadow. [CRYING] I just hope that somehow, I can find myself again. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] What did they do to us? What did they do? Mrs. Eckstein, is there anything you want to share with your grandchildren or their children? Any message?

speaker: interviewee
text: I want to let my grandchildren know that I love them dearly. I love my children very dearly. That's all we have. Please, don't let anybody interfere in your love towards us. Because we don't want anything. We just want them to be close to us. And I hope they will live by it. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I want to let them know that I would do anything for them. And I never want them to feel bad or guilty if they hurt us. They hurt us many times. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I want them to go out in life, make something of themselves, just like Marilyn and Irene. They made it. And they did it on their own, not with our help, not-- we had nothing to do with it. They did it on their own, under very difficult circumstances. And we are very proud of them, very proud. And I wish them good luck in their lives. And, and please, Marilyn, Irene, don't ever feel guilty. Just stay together, no matter what happens, no matter who comes between you two. Stay together. Protect each other, because that's all you have. You have no uncles, no aunts, no nobody. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I love you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you like to stop? Or is there another-- is there a message that you-- you had mentioned that there's something you wanted to say.

speaker: interviewee
text: About what?

speaker: interviewer
text: About your relatives-- is there a message that you wanted to, to say about finding your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: I want to let anybody know, if anybody knows of any relative, no matter where you are, please contact us. And I'll come to see you. My name is Lillian Eckstein. I was born Mendelson, Lily. And I live in New York. Please let me know. Whether you're in Australia, Russia, or wherever. I will come to get you. Please.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, I think we should stop.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.
